does: This paper examines two underexplored aspects of synthetic data curation for post-training: whether filtering decisions are grounded in source evidence and whether rejected samples can be recovered rather than discarded. The authors conduct controlled experiments across different gating configurations, recovery strategies, and model scales using adversarially injected evaluation corpora with ground-truth failure labels. Key findings include that exact source provenance improves faithfulness filtering for stronger judges, that hallucination and reward-based gates reject largely disjoint populations necessitating both, and that an adaptive recovery pipeline combining failure diagnosis with targeted regeneration outperforms naive resampling on multiple metrics including yield, recovery rate, and injection recall. Downstream fine-tuning quality is primarily determined by generator scale, with filtration and recovery conditions providing meaningful secondary contributions.
connects: This work builds on recent literature examining synthetic data quality in large language model training, extending prior research on filtering mechanisms like reward models and LLM judges. It responds to and builds upon concerns about hallucinations and faithfulness in synthetic generation raised in earlier work on synthetic data curation. The paper connects to research on provenance tracking and evidence grounding in neural systems, applying these ideas to the practical problem of identifying why generations fail. It also engages with work on sample recovery and reweighting strategies, contrasting adaptive recovery against simpler baseline approaches. The use of adversarially injected corpora draws on established evaluation methodologies in robustness testing, providing a principled way to measure filtering effectiveness against known failure modes.
enables: This work opens pathways for more sophisticated synthetic data pipelines that maintain bidirectional connections between generated samples and their source evidence. The provenance-grounded approach enables future research into interpretable filtering mechanisms that can explain rejection decisions in terms of underlying source contradictions. The adaptive recovery framework points toward systems that minimize waste in data curation through systematic diagnosis and targeted regeneration, potentially improving sample efficiency in post-training. These findings could inspire development of more nuanced filtering architectures that combine multiple complementary signals rather than single monolithic judges. The work also suggests future investigation into how different failure modes interact, whether different recovery strategies suit different failure types, and how provenance-grounded curation scales to larger synthetic pipelines used in production model training.
